title: HW2 Q6 — Strengthen I.H. for $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$
statement: $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$. Prove by induction that $T(n) = O(n^2)$. Hint: plain I.H. $T(k) \le Ck^2$ fails; strengthen.

steps.title: Step 1 — see why plain I.H. fails.
steps.body:
Try I.H. $T(k) \le Ck^2$ for all $k < n$. Step:

$$T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n \le 4 \cdot C(n/2)^2 + n = Cn^2 + n.$$

We want $\le Cn^2$. But we got $Cn^2 + n$ — there's a leftover $+n$. No constant $C$ can absorb it (adding $n$ is linear, which is much less than $Cn^2$, but the induction demands *exact* inequality).

tips.label: Why 'just a lower order term' isn't enough
tips.content: The point of proving an inductive bound is that the bound cascades. If you prove $T(n) \le Cn^2 + n$, then the next level gets $T(2n) \le 4(Cn^2 + n) + 2n = 4Cn^2 + 6n$, and those linear slack terms compound into $O(n^2)$ extra work over $\log n$ levels — which is fine asymptotically but the induction itself can't conclude $T(n) \le Cn^2$ without a tighter bookkeeping.

steps.title: Step 2 — strengthen the I.H.
steps.body:
Try I.H. $T(k) \le Ck^2 - k$ for all $k < n$. This is a stronger claim (subtracts $k$), which is fine because if it holds, then $T(k) \le Ck^2$ certainly does.

Step:

$$T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n \le 4\left(C(n/2)^2 - n/2\right) + n = Cn^2 - 2n + n = Cn^2 - n.$$

Exactly the strengthened bound at $n$. ✓

tips.label: Why subtract exactly $k$
tips.content: In the inductive step, the factor-of-4 multiplies $-n/2$ into $-2n$. Adding the $+n$ from the recurrence leaves $-n$. That matches the strengthened bound — the subtraction exactly absorbs the recurrence's additive term.

steps.title: Step 3 — base case + conclusion.
steps.body:
**Base $n = 1$:** need $T(1) = 1 \le C - 1$. So $C \ge 2$.

**Conclusion:** for any $C \ge 2$, $T(n) \le Cn^2 - n \le Cn^2$ for all $n \ge 1$. So $T(n) = O(n^2)$. $\square$

tips.label: Tree check
tips.content: Tree: level $i$ has $4^i$ subproblems of size $n/2^i$, non-recursive work $n/2^i$. Level work $= 4^i \cdot n/2^i = n \cdot 2^i$. Increasing geometric, dominated by leaves: $4^{\log_2 n} = n^2$. Confirms $\Theta(n^2)$.

answerKey: Strengthen I.H. to $T(k) \le Ck^2 - k$ (for $C \ge 2$). Inductive step: $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n \le 4(C(n/2)^2 - n/2) + n = Cn^2 - n$. Hence $T(n) = O(n^2)$.
